Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Protective Order And Discovery Stay And Proof Of Service
Off Calendar Jan-07-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (by Zoom):
James Sales, Angela Sales 510-773-9323 Plaintiffs in Pro Per.
Mr. Armanini, Esq. 925-718-1040 Dawson Law Offices for Defendant ALexander Harken
Susan Noble, Esq. 707-523-1181 Clement Fitzpatrick & Kenworthy for Defendant Ajay Hsu Martin et al. who is also present.

Parties state appearance. The Court announces the tentative ruling. The Court states a timely notice of contest the TR was received.

ARGUED. Mr. Armanini contests and argues; 5th amendment rights, disagrees with blanket refusal to participate language, limited in time and scope, ready to present client for deposition, not abuse, cites case authority. Court comments. Ms. Angela Sales responds. AFter full argument, the Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling with a correction that it is Mr. Harken's motion.

1 - Defendant Ajay Hsu Martin's motion for protective order and stay of discovery is DENIED.

Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2025.420, the Court may issue a protective order or stay discovery, but only upon a showing of good cause. Defendant bears the burden of demonstrating that such relief is necessary to prevent unwarranted annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden. These remedies are extraordinary and are not intended to categorically shield a party from discovery. (In re Providian Credit Card Cases (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 292, 298-299.)

Defendant failed to meet its burden of demonstrating good cause for entry of a protective order. Defendant's sole basis for seeking both a protective order and a discovery stay is the Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination. While the privilege is an important constitutional protection, it does not permit a blanket refusal to participate in discovery. To justify either form of relief he now requests, Defendant must demonstrate a reasonable fear of prosecution based on a real and appreciable risk, not speculative or hypothetical concerns. (Troy v. Superior Court (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1006, 1011; United States v. Jones (10th Cir. 1983) 703 F.2d 473, 476.) Defendant presents no evidence of pending criminal charges or an active or threatened criminal investigation. There is no indication that law enforcement has initiated proceedings or that prosecution is imminent. Under these circumstances, the asserted risk of self-incrimination is speculative and does not justify either a protective order or a stay of discovery. (In re Marriage of Sachs (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 1144, 1149-1152.)

The court has considered Defendant's Fifth Amendment concerns and balanced them against Plaintiff's right to obtain discovery necessary to prosecute this action. ON this record, the balance tips decisively in favor of Plaintiff. In the absence of a concrete risk of criminal prosecution, granting a protective order or staying discovery is unwarranted.

Order is signed.

MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiffs Angela Aquino-Sales and James Sales' unopposed motion to file first amended complaint is GRANTED.

There is a liberal policy in favor of amendment. (See Nestle v. Santa Monica (1972) 6 Cal.3d 920, 939.) Code of Civil Procedure section 576 states that "[a]ny judge, at any time before or after commencement of trial, in the furtherance of justice, and upon such terms as may be proper, may allow the amendment of any pleading or pretrial conference order." Because judicial policy favors resolution of all disputed matters between the parties in the same lawsuit, the court's discretion will usually be exercised liberally to permit amendment of the pleadings. (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 6:638 (Rutter 2023); see also Nestle v. Santa Monica (1972) 6 Cal.3d 920, 939.) Indeed, "it is an abuse of discretion to deny leave to amend where the opposing party was not misled or prejudiced by the amendment." (Kittredge Sports Co. v. Superior Court (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1045, 1048.)

Here, Plaintiffs' have established good cause to authorize the filing of the first amended complaint.

Plaintiffs must file the first amended complaint within 7 days of this order. Plaintiffs must promptly serve the first amended complaint as well.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.

PLAINTIFF ANGELA AQUINO-SALES, JAMES SALES To Compel Responses To General Interrogatories And Special Interrogatories, Set One From Defendant Ajay Hsu Martin

This is a wrongful death action arising out of the 2020 death of Jaxon Sales. From the pleadings, it appears the decedent visited the apartment of defendant Harken at 399 Fremont Street. The plaintiffs, appearing in propria persona, allege that Harken, with the assistance and/or cooperation of defendant Martin, provided the decedent with contraband substances that led to his death. Plaintiffs seek damages arising from their loss of love, care and companionship of the decedent.

On January 31, 2024, plaintiffs served general and special interrogatories on defendant Martin. On March 4, 2024, plaintiffs filed a motion to compel further responses from Martin on the ground that his responses to plaintiffs' interrogatories were incomplete and evasive. Defendant Martin opposes the plaintiffs' motion on the ground, inter alia, that plaintiffs failed to file a separate statement with their motion per the Rules of Court. Nor did they file a concise summary of the interrogatories in dispute and to which their motion is directed.

The absence of the separate statement makes it impossible for the Court to review the questions and answers upon which it is being asked to rule. Similarly, it is impossible for the Court to determine the merits of plaintiffs' questions and the sufficiency of Mr. Martin's responses. Accordingly, it is ruled that plaintiffs' motion to compel further responses to interrogatories by defendant Martin is DENIED, albeit without prejudice to the motion being renewed in compliance with all governing provisions of the Rules of Court and Code of Civil Procedure. The appearance of plaintiffs without an attorney does not relieve them of their obligation to comply with those governing provisions.

Motion denied without prejudice. The moving papers state that the discovery was served by mail on April 20, 2023 which means the responding party has until May 25, 2023 to serve responses.
